perturbation.original_text: In any event, the Receiving Party agrees to undertake reasonable precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material and the Discussion Information, the Receiving Party hereby accepts responsibility for any breach of this letter agreement by any of its Representatives (it being understood that such responsibility shall be in addition to and not by way of limitation of any right or remedy that the Company may have against such Representatives), and the Receiving Party agrees at its sole expense to take all reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Evaluation Material and the Discussion Information.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, the Receiving Party agrees to undertake precautions to safeguard and protect the confidentiality of the Evaluation Material and the Discussion Information, the Receiving Party hereby accepts responsibility for any breach of this letter agreement by any of its Representatives (it being understood that such responsibility shall be in addition to and not by way of limitation of any right or remedy that the Company may have against such Representatives), and the Receiving Party agrees at its sole expense to attempt to restrain its Representatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or uses of the Evaluation Material and the Discussion Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'reasonable precautions' to 'precautions' removes the standard of reasonableness, making the required level of security undefined. Replacing 'take all reasonable measures' with 'attempt to restrain' weakens the obligation to prevent unauthorized disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa requires 'reasonable security measures' to protect private information. Removing 'reasonable' and weakening the obligation to 'attempt to restrain' conflicts with the requirement to implement appropriate safeguards against unauthorized access.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'reasonable precautions' and taking 'all reasonable measures' to protect confidentiality. The changed text weakens these obligations by removing the 'reasonable' qualifier and changing 'take all reasonable measures' to 'attempt to restrain.' The Law Explanation states that NY Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa requires 'reasonable security measures' to protect private information. By removing 'reasonable' and weakening the obligation, the modified contract potentially fails to meet the legally required standard of 'reasonable security measures,' creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 also refers to data security protections under NY law, reinforcing the importance of adhering to specific legal standards for data security.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, the Receiving Party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will not disclose to any other person the fact that the Evaluation Material has been made available to the Receiving Party and July 1, 2005 Page 3 of 8 its Representatives, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a Possible Transaction or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto, including the status thereof and the identity of the parties thereto (collectively, the “ Discussion Information ”); provided that, in the event the Receiving Party becomes obligated to make public disclosure of Discussion Information pursuant to its obligations under 1934 Act or any other applicable law, rule or regulation, the Receiving Party shall, except as otherwise consented to by the Disclosing Party, limit such disclosure to the minimum disclosure so required and, without limiting the foregoing, to the extent the Receiving Party has any discretion with respect thereto, the Receiving Party shall not disclose any possible terms or of any possible transaction involving the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, the Receiving Party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will endeavor not to disclose to any other person the fact that the Evaluation Material has been made available to the Receiving Party and July 1, 2005 Page 3 of 8 its Representatives, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a Possible Transaction or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto, including the status thereof and the identity of the parties thereto (collectively, the “ Discussion Information ”); provided that, in the event the Receiving Party becomes obligated to make public disclosure of Discussion Information pursuant to its obligations under 1934 Act or any other applicable law, rule or regulation, the Receiving Party should limit such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not disclose' to 'will endeavor not to disclose' weakens the prohibition on disclosure, creating ambiguity about the Receiving Party's obligation. Furthermore, modifying 'the Receiving Party shall limit such disclosure' to 'the Receiving Party should limit such disclosure' weakens the mandatory obligation to limit disclosure to a suggestion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The ambiguity introduced allows for potential selective disclosure of information, contradicting the requirements for fair and equal access to material non-public information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's obligation to not disclose information, weakening the original prohibition. The change from 'will not disclose' to 'will endeavor not to disclose' and 'shall limit' to 'should limit' creates a less stringent requirement. According to 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, it is unlawful to employ any device to defraud or omit a material fact, which the ambiguity in disclosure obligations could facilitate. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the legal requirement for fair and equal access to information by creating potential for selective disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party understands, acknowledges and agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material or the Discussion Information. The Receiving Party agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives shall have any liability to the Receiving Party or to any of its Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of the Evaluation Material or any errors therein or omissions therefrom. Only those representations or warranties which are made in a final definitive agreement regarding any transactions contemplated hereby, when, as and if executed, and subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be specified therein, will have any legal effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party understands, acknowledges and agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the general accuracy of the Evaluation Material or the Discussion Information. The Receiving Party agrees that neither the Disclosing Party nor any of its Representatives shall have limited liability to the Receiving Party or to any of its Representatives relating to or resulting from the use of the Evaluation Material or any errors therein or omissions therefrom. Only those representations or warranties which are made in a final definitive agreement regarding any transactions contemplated hereby, when, as and if executed, and subject to such limitations and restrictions as may be specified therein, will have any legal effect.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'accuracy or completeness' to 'general accuracy' introduces ambiguity about the level of accuracy expected, and opens up possible challenges based on the standard. Changing 'shall have any liability' to 'shall have limited liability' introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of the liability, which contradicts standard practices for disclosures of financial condition, operations, assets, and liabilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Business Law Article 26, Section 352-c
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Gen. Bus. Law § 352-c prohibits fraudulent practices in the offer, sale, or purchase of securities. By disclaiming all liability for the accuracy or completeness of evaluation material, the original agreement aims to protect the disclosing party. Modifying this to disclaim only 'general accuracy' and introducing 'limited liability' creates ambiguity and could allow for the intentional or negligent dissemination of inaccurate information without clear accountability, thereby potentially facilitating fraudulent practices.
perturbation.justification: The original text disclaims 'any liability,' whereas the changed text disclaims 'limited liability'. This modification introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of liability. The Law Explanation states that this change 'could allow for the intentional or negligent dissemination of inaccurate information without clear accountability, thereby potentially facilitating fraudulent practices.' The scraped snippet points to NY Gen. Bus. Law Article 23-A which concerns 'Fraudulent Practices In Respect to Stocks, Bonds and Other Securities'. Although the snippet does not provide the text of the law, the combination of the Law Explanation and the title of Article 23-A suggests that limiting liability in this context could contradict regulations against fraudulent practices, as it obscures the accountability for inaccurate information.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party hereby agrees that, for a period of one year from the date hereof, neither the Receiving Party nor any of its Representatives will solicit to employ (i) any of the officers of the Disclosing Party or (ii) any of the employees of the Disclosing Party with whom the Receiving Party has contact or who are specifically identified to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing party or any of its Representatives for purposes hereof during the period of the Receiving Party’s investigation of the Disclosing Party, in either case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided , however , that the Receiving Party may engage in general solicitations for employees in the ordinary course of business and consistent with past practice and that the Receiving Party may solicit or employ any employee of the Disclosing Party six months after such party’s employment has been terminated by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party hereby agrees that, for a period of one year from the date hereof, neither the Receiving Party nor any of its Representatives will actively solicit to employ (i) any of the officers of the Disclosing Party or (ii) any of the employees of the Disclosing Party with whom the Receiving Party has contact or who are specifically identified to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing party or any of its Representatives for purposes hereof during the period of the Receiving Party’s investigation of the Disclosing Party, in either case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided , however , that the Receiving Party may engage in general solicitations for employees and that the Receiving Party may solicit or employ any employee of the Disclosing Party after such party’s employment has been terminated by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'actively' before 'solicit' makes the restriction less clear and easier to circumvent, which contradicts restrictions on non-solicitation agreements. Removing the six-month requirement is also a violation of New York state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Labor Law § 201-d protects employees' rights to engage in legal recreational activities outside of work, including seeking new employment. Overly broad non-solicitation agreements can be construed as restricting this right, particularly if they are not limited in scope and duration. Removing 'consistent with past practice' and the six-month restriction on hiring employees after termination strengthens the non-solicitation clause which can impede former employees' ability to find new jobs, thus conflicting with NY Labor Law § 201-d.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the non-solicitation agreement by adding 'actively' and removing the six-month waiting period after termination. The Law Explanation states that removing the six-month restriction strengthens the non-solicitation clause and impedes former employees' ability to find new jobs, thus conflicting with NY Labor Law § 201-d. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the table of contents for NY labor laws including Article 7 which includes sections 201-C, 201-D, and 201-E, and the Law explanation references 201-D, indicating that the changes in the contract potentially violate employee rights as understood within New York labor law, specifically related to limitations on non-solicitation agreements which can restrict former employees from seeking new employment.
